agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with an established production domain model governing Shipment-to-Carrier assignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically decompose overweight shipments (>500kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, without supervisor approval. This request directly conflicts with two foundational invariants of the existing model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported. The existing model enforces that a Shipment has exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to move a Shipment. Assignment is locked once made."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"The only role authorized to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment. This is a domain-level access control invariant, not a UI preference."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by decomposing a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"The proposed automatic decomposition of a Shipment exceeding a weight threshold into multiple sub-shipments with independent carrier assignments. Contradicts existing invariants."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The system guarantees every Shipment has exactly one Carrier at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. If a Shipment has zero or multiple Carriers, accountability is undefined — who is liable for loss, who receives payment, who is tracked? The system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and accountability constraint. Unrestricted reassignment creates audit gaps, liability disputes, and potential fraud vectors. Supervisor gating is not a convenience policy — it is an integrity control.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change Carrier assignment without Supervisor authorization must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — a shipment on a truck is on exactly one truck operated by exactly one carrier. This is the real-world truth the model encodes.","testable_assertion":"No Shipment may transition to 'dispatched' status unless it has exactly one Carrier assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Auto-split creates sub-shipments but one sub-shipment fails carrier assignment — parent shipment is now partially split with orphaned freight.","expected_behavior":"Cannot be defined without resolving the invariant contradiction. Under current rules, this state is illegal."},{"scenario":"A shipment weighing exactly 500kg — is this at-or-above or strictly-above the threshold?","expected_behavior":"Threshold boundary must be explicitly defined (>500 vs >=500). This is a policy, not an invariant."},{"scenario":"A split sub-shipment needs carrier reassignment — does the supervisor rule apply to sub-shipments or only original shipments?","expected_behavior":"Cannot be defined. If sub-shipments are Shipments, BR-2 applies. If they are a new entity type, the entire domain model needs re-examination."},{"scenario":"Customer queries tracking for original shipment that has been split into 3 sub-shipments across 3 carriers.","expected_behavior":"Current model has no concept of parent-child shipment relationships. Customer-facing tracking, invoicing, liability, and SLA attribution are all undefined."},{"scenario":"Auto-split assigns carrier B to a sub-shipment, but carrier B is later found unsuitable — supervisor must reassign per BR-2, but supervisor never approved the original assignment.","expected_behavior":"Contradiction: system auto-assigned without supervisor, but supervisor owns reassignment. Accountability chain is broken."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Splitting shipments across carriers changes cost structures (multiple pickups, multiple deliveries, potential volume discount loss). Invoicing must handle parent-child relationships. Liability allocation per sub-shipment is undefined.","operational":"Introduces significant complexity: split orchestration, partial failure handling, multi-carrier coordination for a single customer order. Dispatch, tracking, and exception handling all need rearchitecting.","user":"Customers expect one shipment = one tracking number = one point of accountability. Splitting silently may confuse customers and erode trust. Support teams need tooling to view the full picture.","risk":"HIGH. This feature as specified would violate two production invariants. Shipping it as described could corrupt system state, create liability gaps, and break downstream processes (billing, tracking, SLA enforcement) that depend on the one-shipment-one-carrier guarantee.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must coordinate multi-carrier pickups for what was a single shipment. Exception handling (delays, damage, loss) becomes multi-party."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their gating authority is bypassed for auto-split assignments. Accountability model is undermined."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing, cost allocation, and carrier payment structures must handle split shipments — none of this exists today."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Tracking experience changes. One order may now produce multiple tracking numbers. SLA enforcement per sub-shipment vs original shipment is undefined."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive partial loads from split shipments. Contractual terms may not cover partial shipment liability."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain of custody documentation, liability allocation, and insurance coverage must be re-evaluated for split shipments."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between the requested feature and two established production invariants (one-carrier-per-shipment and supervisor-only reassignment). Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to relax these invariants or introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup), acceptance criteria cannot be responsibly defined. The 500kg threshold itself is a policy detail that is secondary to the structural conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and BR-1/BR-2 is unambiguous","The feature as described cannot be implemented without violating production invariants","Stakeholder impact is broad and significant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether product team intends to introduce a new aggregate (ShipmentGroup) or relax existing invariants","Whether the 500kg threshold is firm or configurable","Whether sub-shipments should be full Shipment entities or a new bounded concept","Whether supervisor bypass is intentional policy change or an oversight in the request"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and its invariants are intentional and battle-tested in production","The product team may not have been fully aware of the invariants they are asking to violate"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment). If a Shipment is split into sub-shipments each with a different carrier, either: (A) sub-shipments are Shipments, violating the one-carrier-per-shipment invariant on the original, or (B) sub-shipments are a new entity type, requiring a new aggregate design that does not yet exist.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Finance","Customers","Carriers","Legal"],"options":["A: Introduce a new ShipmentGroup aggregate that contains multiple Shipments, each respecting BR-1. The original 'shipment' becomes a ShipmentGroup, and each sub-shipment is a first-class Shipment with exactly one Carrier.","B: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments with a lead-carrier concept. This is a fundamental model change with massive downstream impact.","C: Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model and propose an alternative (e.g., manual supervisor-initiated split)."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves all existing invariants by introducing a parent aggregate (ShipmentGroup) while allowing each child Shipment to have exactly one Carrier. The auto-split becomes a ShipmentGroup creation operation, not a Shipment mutation."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses supervisor-only carrier assignment (BR-2). Auto-assigning carriers to sub-shipments without supervisor involvement directly violates the locked-assignment invariant.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Operations","Legal"],"options":["A: Auto-split proposes assignments but requires supervisor approval before dispatch (preserves BR-2 intent).","B: Create a new 'system-initiated assignment' authorization level parallel to supervisor, with full audit trail.","C: Accept that auto-split is a policy exception to BR-2 and carve out an explicit exemption with compensating controls (audit log, post-hoc supervisor review)."],"recommendation":"Option A. Auto-split should propose, not execute. Supervisor approval preserves the accountability chain. If speed is critical, Option C with compensating controls is acceptable but requires explicit sign-off from operations leadership."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authorization for carrier assignment","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"Introduces a parent-child shipment concept that does not exist in the domain model, with undefined behavior for tracking, billing, SLA, and liability","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-3: The one-shipment-one-carrier-at-dispatch guarantee is the foundation for downstream processes"}]}
